questions.question_text: A two-way table shows that 80% of athletes prefer sports drinks while only 40% of non-athletes prefer sports drinks. Is there an association between being an athlete and preferring sports drinks?

options.text: No, the percentages are unrelated
Yes, because the conditional distributions differ significantly between the two groups
No, because both groups drink sports drinks
There is not enough information

questions.explanation: When the conditional relative frequencies differ substantially between groups (80% vs. 40%), it indicates an association between the variables.

questions.question_text: If two categorical variables are independent, what should be true about their conditional relative frequency distributions?

options.text: They should be very different from each other
They should be approximately the same across all categories
One distribution should be all zeros
They should add up to 2

questions.explanation: If two variables are independent, knowing the category of one variable does not change the distribution of the other. The conditional distributions will be approximately equal.

questions.question_text: A table shows: Boys who like pizza = 60%, Boys who like tacos = 40%, Girls who like pizza = 58%, Girls who like tacos = 42%. Is there strong evidence of an association?

options.text: Yes, the differences are very large
No, the conditional distributions are nearly identical, suggesting little or no association
Yes, because boys like pizza more
There is no way to determine association from percentages

questions.explanation: The conditional distributions are very close (60% vs. 58% for pizza), suggesting the food preference is essentially the same regardless of gender, indicating little or no association.

questions.question_text: In a study of 200 people, 90% of dog owners exercise daily while 50% of non-dog owners exercise daily. What does this suggest?

options.text: There is likely an association between dog ownership and daily exercise
Dog ownership causes exercise
There is no association
Exercise causes dog ownership

questions.explanation: The large difference in conditional frequencies (90% vs. 50%) suggests a strong association between dog ownership and daily exercise, though association does not prove causation.

questions.question_text: A school examines the relationship between grade level (9th or 12th) and whether students have a part-time job. The conditional frequency of having a job is 15% for 9th graders and 55% for 12th graders. What does this indicate?

options.text: No association between grade level and employment
A strong association between grade level and having a part-time job
12th graders are lazier
9th graders work more

questions.explanation: The large difference (15% vs. 55%) between the conditional frequencies indicates a strong association between grade level and employment status.

questions.question_text: Which of the following would indicate NO association between two categorical variables in a two-way table?

options.text: The conditional relative frequencies for each row are dramatically different
The conditional relative frequencies for each row are approximately the same
One row has much higher frequencies than the other
The grand total is an odd number

questions.explanation: No association means the distribution of one variable is the same regardless of which category of the other variable you examine. The conditional distributions should be approximately equal.

questions.question_text: A survey finds that 70% of city residents support a new park and 68% of rural residents support it. Is there a strong association between location and park support?

options.text: Yes, city residents are much more supportive
No, the conditional frequencies are very similar, suggesting little to no association
Yes, rural residents oppose the park
Not enough data

questions.explanation: The conditional frequencies are nearly identical (70% vs. 68%), so there is little evidence of an association between location and park support.

questions.question_text: A researcher calculates the expected frequency for a cell in a two-way table using the formula: (row total x column total) / grand total. The row total is 60, the column total is 80, and the grand total is 200. What is the expected frequency?
questions.explanation: Expected frequency = (60 x 80) / 200 = 4800 / 200 = 24.

questions.question_text: Two variables show similar conditional relative frequency distributions across all categories. This suggests the variables are most likely:

options.text: Strongly associated
Independent
Negatively correlated
Causally related

questions.explanation: When conditional distributions are similar across all categories, the variables do not influence each other, suggesting independence.

questions.question_text: A two-way table shows a large difference between the observed frequency and the expected frequency for one cell. What does this suggest?

options.text: The two variables are likely independent
There is likely an association between the two variables
The data was collected incorrectly
The grand total is wrong

questions.explanation: When observed frequencies differ substantially from expected frequencies (those computed assuming independence), it suggests the variables are associated.

questions.question_text: What does 'association' mean in the context of categorical data?

options.text: One variable causes a change in the other
There is a relationship between two categorical variables where the distribution of one variable differs depending on the category of the other
Two variables are always independent
The variables are measured on a numerical scale

questions.explanation: Association in categorical data means that the distribution of one variable changes depending on the category of the other variable, indicating a relationship between them.

questions.question_text: What does 'independence' mean for two categorical variables?

options.text: Knowing the category of one variable provides information about the other
The distribution of one variable is the same regardless of the category of the other variable
The variables are perfectly correlated
Both variables have the same number of categories

questions.explanation: Two categorical variables are independent if the conditional distribution of one variable is the same no matter which category of the other variable is considered.

questions.question_text: What does 'conditional distribution' mean?

options.text: The overall distribution of a single variable
The distribution of one variable within a specific category of another variable
The total number of observations in the study
The difference between two marginal frequencies

questions.explanation: A conditional distribution shows how one variable is distributed when we restrict attention to a specific category of the other variable.

questions.question_text: What does 'expected frequency' mean?

options.text: The frequency that would occur in a cell if the two variables were independent
The actual observed count in a cell
The highest frequency in the table
The frequency predicted by a regression model

questions.explanation: Expected frequency is the count a cell would have if the two variables were completely independent. It is calculated as (row total x column total) / grand total.

questions.question_text: What does 'segmented bar graph' mean?

options.text: A bar graph where each bar is divided into segments representing different categories of a second variable
A bar graph with gaps between the bars
A scatter plot with bars instead of dots
A bar graph that only shows one variable

questions.explanation: A segmented (or stacked) bar graph divides each bar into colored segments representing the proportions of a second categorical variable, making it easy to compare distributions.

questions.question_text: What does 'side-by-side bar graph' mean in the context of categorical data?

options.text: A graph where bars for different categories are placed next to each other for comparison
A graph with only one bar per category
A scatter plot with vertical lines
A table converted into a graph

questions.explanation: A side-by-side bar graph places bars for different subcategories next to each other within each main category, allowing visual comparison of distributions.

questions.question_text: What does 'statistical significance' mean in the context of association?

options.text: The observed difference is large enough that it is unlikely to have occurred by chance alone
The data is accurate
The sample size is very large
The association proves causation

questions.explanation: Statistical significance means the observed association is strong enough that it is unlikely to be due to random chance alone, providing evidence of a real relationship.

questions.question_text: What does 'marginal distribution' mean?

options.text: The distribution of one variable ignoring the other variable, found using the row or column totals
The distribution within a single cell
The distribution of the grand total
A distribution that always sums to zero

questions.explanation: A marginal distribution shows the distribution of a single variable (using row or column totals) without regard to the categories of the other variable.

questions.question_text: What does 'bivariate categorical data' mean?

options.text: Data involving a single categorical variable
Data involving two categorical variables measured on the same set of individuals
Data involving two numerical variables
Data that cannot be put into a table

questions.explanation: Bivariate categorical data involves measuring two categorical variables on the same set of subjects, allowing analysis of the relationship between them.

questions.question_text: What does 'Simpson's Paradox' refer to?

options.text: A trend that appears in overall data but reverses when the data is divided into subgroups
A calculation error that occurs with large data sets
The tendency for correlation to imply causation
A pattern that only appears in categorical data

questions.explanation: Simpson's Paradox occurs when a trend seen in aggregated data reverses or disappears when the data is separated into subgroups, often due to a lurking variable.

questions.question_text: A marketing team surveys 400 customers about brand preference (Brand A or Brand B) and age group (18-30 or 31-50). Results: 18-30 & Brand A = 100, 18-30 & Brand B = 60, 31-50 & Brand A = 90, 31-50 & Brand B = 150. Is there an association between age group and brand preference?

options.text: Yes; 18-30 year-olds prefer Brand A at 62.5% while 31-50 year-olds prefer Brand A at only 37.5%
No; both age groups prefer Brand A equally
No; the differences are too small to matter
Yes; both age groups prefer Brand B

questions.explanation: 18-30: 100/160 = 62.5% prefer Brand A. 31-50: 90/240 = 37.5% prefer Brand A. The substantial difference in conditional frequencies indicates an association.

questions.question_text: A school examines whether there is an association between gender (male or female) and preferred learning style (visual or auditory). Results: Male & Visual = 45, Male & Auditory = 55, Female & Visual = 48, Female & Auditory = 52. Is there an association?

options.text: Yes, a strong association exists
No, the conditional distributions are nearly identical (45% vs. 48% visual), suggesting no meaningful association
Yes, females strongly prefer visual learning
The sample size is too small to determine

questions.explanation: Males: 45/100 = 45% visual. Females: 48/100 = 48% visual. The conditional distributions are nearly identical, suggesting no meaningful association.

questions.question_text: A health clinic collects data on flu vaccination status (vaccinated or not) and whether patients got the flu (yes or no). Results: Vaccinated & Flu = 10, Vaccinated & No Flu = 190, Not Vaccinated & Flu = 60, Not Vaccinated & No Flu = 140. Is vaccination status associated with getting the flu?

options.text: No, vaccination has no effect
Yes; only 5% of vaccinated patients got the flu compared to 30% of unvaccinated patients
Yes; vaccinated patients got the flu more often
The data cannot determine association

questions.explanation: Vaccinated flu rate: 10/200 = 5%. Unvaccinated flu rate: 60/200 = 30%. The large difference indicates a strong association between vaccination and flu occurrence.

questions.question_text: A college admissions study looks at acceptance rates by gender. Overall, males are accepted at 40% and females at 35%. But when examined by department, females have higher acceptance rates in every department. This is an example of:

options.text: Positive correlation
Simpson's Paradox
Random variation
Negative correlation

questions.explanation: This is Simpson's Paradox: the overall trend (males accepted at a higher rate) reverses in every subgroup (females accepted at a higher rate in each department). This happens because females apply more to competitive departments.

questions.question_text: A car insurance company compares claim rates by car color (red or blue) and driving record (clean or violations). Results: Red & Clean = 80, Red & Violations = 20, Blue & Clean = 160, Blue & Violations = 40. Is car color associated with driving violations?

options.text: Yes; red car owners have more violations
No; both groups have 20% violation rates, indicating no association
Yes; blue car owners have more violations
The data is insufficient

questions.explanation: Red: 20/100 = 20% violations. Blue: 40/200 = 20% violations. The conditional distributions are identical, indicating no association between car color and driving violations.

questions.question_text: A restaurant chain examines customer satisfaction (satisfied or not) at two locations. Location A: 150 satisfied, 50 not. Location B: 100 satisfied, 100 not. A manager creates a segmented bar graph. What would the graph reveal?

options.text: Both locations have the same satisfaction rate
Location A has 75% satisfaction while Location B has 50%, showing an association between location and satisfaction
Location B has higher satisfaction
No patterns can be observed

questions.explanation: Location A: 150/200 = 75% satisfied. Location B: 100/200 = 50% satisfied. The segmented bar graph would clearly show the different proportions, indicating an association.

questions.question_text: A school district examines graduation rates by program (traditional or alternative) and socioeconomic status (low or high income). Overall, traditional programs have higher graduation rates. But within each income group, alternative programs have higher rates. What explains this reversal?

options.text: Data entry error
Simpson's Paradox: alternative programs enroll a higher proportion of low-income students who have lower overall graduation rates
Traditional programs are simply better
The sample sizes are too different

questions.explanation: This is Simpson's Paradox. Alternative programs serve a disproportionately larger share of low-income students, who tend to have lower overall graduation rates, which lowers the aggregate rate despite higher within-group performance.

questions.question_text: A phone manufacturer surveys 500 users about phone preference (Model X or Model Y) and usage pattern (heavy or light). Results: Heavy & X = 130, Heavy & Y = 70, Light & X = 120, Light & Y = 180. Does usage pattern appear to be associated with phone preference?

options.text: Yes; 65% of heavy users prefer X while only 40% of light users prefer X
No; both groups have similar preferences
Yes; light users overwhelmingly prefer X
The data shows independence

questions.explanation: Heavy users: 130/200 = 65% prefer X. Light users: 120/300 = 40% prefer X. The substantial difference in conditional distributions indicates an association.

questions.question_text: A researcher examines whether handedness (left or right) is associated with career field (STEM or non-STEM) among 600 professionals. Results: Left & STEM = 18, Left & Non-STEM = 42, Right & STEM = 162, Right & Non-STEM = 378. Is there an association?

options.text: Yes; left-handed people are more likely to be in STEM
No; both groups have a 30% STEM rate, suggesting independence
Yes; right-handed people dominate STEM
The sample size is too small to determine

questions.explanation: Left-handed STEM rate: 18/60 = 30%. Right-handed STEM rate: 162/540 = 30%. The identical conditional distributions indicate no association between handedness and career field.

questions.question_text: A community center offers two after-school programs (tutoring or sports). Parents are surveyed about satisfaction (satisfied or not) by program. Results: Tutoring & Satisfied = 60, Tutoring & Not = 40, Sports & Satisfied = 90, Sports & Not = 10. The center director says both programs are equally well-received. Is the director correct?

options.text: Yes, both programs have similar satisfaction
No; tutoring has 60% satisfaction while sports has 90% satisfaction, showing an association between program type and satisfaction
Yes, since the total number of satisfied parents (150) is high
The data is inconclusive

questions.explanation: Tutoring satisfaction: 60/100 = 60%. Sports satisfaction: 90/100 = 90%. The large difference shows a clear association between program type and satisfaction level. The director is not correct.
